metadata.career_relevance: Parameterized tests are how professional teams achieve thorough test coverage efficiently. Instead of writing 10 identical test methods that differ only in input, you write one test that runs against 10 inputs. This is how boundary bugs are caught — the off-by-one in a discount tier, the null handling in a parser, the empty-list edge case in a search. After this topic, your tests are exhaustive without being repetitive.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll use @ValueSource, @CsvSource, @MethodSource, @NullAndEmptySource, and @EnumSource to run tests against many inputs, systematically test boundary values and edge cases, and write exhaustive test coverage without duplication.

visual_aid.description: Three panels: (1) @CsvSource structure: table with header row (param1 | param2 | expected) and 5 data rows, arrow pointing to one test method with those parameters. JUnit expands to 5 test runs shown as 5 green check marks. (2) Boundary value diagram: number line showing 0→9 (no discount zone), 10→49 (10% zone), 50+ (20% zone). Red X marks at: 9, 10, 49, 50 labeled as boundary values to test. Green check marks at: 1 (middle of zone 1), 25 (middle of zone 2), 75 (middle of zone 3). (3) @MethodSource: static method returning Stream<Arguments> arrow into @ParameterizedTest, which fans out to N test executions.
visual_aid.alt_text: CsvSource table to parameterized test, boundary value zones on number line, MethodSource stream to test executions

worked_example.scenario: Test an `OrderValidator` class that validates Flipkart-style orders. Uses @CsvSource for multiple input combinations, @MethodSource for complex Product objects, @NullAndEmptySource for null handling, and boundary value analysis for quantity limits.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Test `OrderValidator.validate(Order order)` which validates:
- Order must not be null
- Product name must not be blank
- Quantity must be 1–999
- Price must be > 0 and < 1,000,000
- For quantity >= 10, a discount tier must be specified
worked_example.expected_output:
Test Results:
✓ isValid_csvCases (7 cases)
✓ validate_qtyBoundary (6 cases)
✓ validate_nullOrEmptyProductName_fails (2 cases — null and empty)
✓ validate_nullOrder_throwsNPE

16 test executions, all passed
things_to_notice: @ParameterizedTest expands to N individual test executions — each shows in the test report separately. If case 5 fails, JUnit tells you exactly which input caused it.
@CsvSource uses comma as separator. If a field itself contains a comma, wrap it in single quotes: `'Smith, John'`. Empty strings in CSV: `''` (two single quotes).

@NullAndEmptySource is two executions: one with null, one with empty string `""`. It's shorthand for `@ValueSource(strings = {null, ""})`. Combine with @ValueSource(strings = {" ", "  "}) for blank strings too.

The parameterized test name: `@ParameterizedTest(name = "[{index}] qty={0}")` makes the test report say 'qty=10' instead of '[2]'. {0} refers to the first parameter, {1} to the second, etc. Clear names help when a specific case fails.

Boundary value testing (0, 1, 998, 999, 1000 for a 1-999 range) catches off-by-one errors that are extremely common in production code. The boundary values ALWAYS catch if a developer wrote `> 999` instead of `>= 1000`.

industry_examples.use_case: Razorpay's API validates payment amounts in paise (₹1 = 100 paise). A @CsvSource test covers: 0 (invalid), 1 (minimum valid), 100 (₹1), 100000000 (₹10 lakh — maximum), 100000001 (above max — invalid), -1 (negative). Six cases, one test method. Every boundary covered. The off-by-one in the maximum check would be caught immediately.

@NullAndEmptySource + @ValueSource(strings = {" ", "\t"}) tests that blank searches return empty results gracefully instead of throwing or returning random products. @MethodSource provides complex Product objects for filter tests. @CsvSource maps category + price range → expected result count. These tests run on every commit.

India has multiple GST slabs: 0%, 5%, 12%, 18%, 28%. @EnumSource(GSTCategory.class) tests that every category maps to a valid rate. @CsvSource tests that the correct rate is applied for each category. Boundary tests verify that the rate changes exactly at category boundaries, not one item off.

applications.interview_relevance:
Parameterized testing questions in senior interviews:

- **'How do you test boundary values?'** — Test: just below boundary, at boundary exactly, just above boundary. For 1-999 range: 0, 1, 2, 998, 999, 1000.
- **'How do you avoid writing the same test 10 times?'** → @ParameterizedTest with @CsvSource — one test method, many inputs.
- **'What is equivalence partitioning?'** → Group inputs that produce the same output (partition). Test one input from each partition. Boundaries between partitions are critical.
- **'How do you test null handling?'** → @NullAndEmptySource + assertDoesNotThrow or assertThrows.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily parameterized testing patterns:

- Input validation: @CsvSource with valid/invalid values + expected boolean
- Boundary analysis: @ValueSource with n-1, n, n+1 at each boundary
- Null/empty: @NullAndEmptySource on any method accepting String
- Complex objects: @MethodSource returning Stream<Arguments>
- All enum values: @EnumSource for exhaustive enum coverage

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Writing `"Smith, John",95000` in @CsvSource — the comma inside Smith, John splits it into two fields.
common_gaps.remediation: Use single quotes for fields containing commas: `"'Smith, John',95000"`. Single quotes inside @CsvSource strings wrap a field that contains the CSV delimiter.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Too many parameters error; parameter types don't match

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using @ValueSource(strings = {"a", "b"}) and forgetting null is a valid input — missing null test coverage.
common_gaps.remediation: Add @NullAndEmptySource alongside @ValueSource, or use @ValueSource with `{null, "", "valid"}` (null literal allowed). Never assume callers won't pass null — always test null explicitly.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: NullPointerException in production from null input that wasn't tested

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Testing only one value from each partition instead of at boundaries — missing the exact boundary value.
common_gaps.remediation: For every rule `qty >= N`, test N-1 (fails), N (passes), N+1 (passes). For `qty < M`, test M-1 (passes), M (fails), M+1 (fails). The boundary value itself is the most likely bug location.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Test passes with value 11 but would fail at exactly 10 (or vice versa)

key_takeaways: @ParameterizedTest: one test method, N executions with different inputs
@ValueSource: single parameter of one type (int, String, double...)
@CsvSource: multiple parameters per row; use single quotes for comma-containing fields
@MethodSource: complex objects; static method returning Stream<Arguments>
@NullAndEmptySource: two executions — null and empty string
@EnumSource: one execution per enum value (or subset)
Boundary value analysis: test n-1, n, n+1 at every boundary
Equivalence partitioning: one test per partition; test boundaries between partitions
Always test null inputs on methods that accept reference types
Empty collection and empty string are different edge cases from null
@ParameterizedTest(name = '...{0}...') for readable test names in reports

self_check_questions.question: A method accepts `String city`. What inputs should you test with @NullAndEmptySource and @ValueSource combined?
self_check_questions.answer: Use both annotations together: null (NullAndEmptySource), empty string '' (NullAndEmptySource), ' ' (single space — ValueSource), 'Hyderabad' (valid, ValueSource), 'New Delhi' (space in name), 'Mumbai' (valid). These cover: null reference, empty string, blank string, normal input, input with special characters. All should be handled gracefully — either processed correctly or throwing a clear exception.

self_check_questions.question: For a discount rule 'GOLD if spending > ₹10,000, SILVER if > ₹5,000, BRONZE otherwise', what are the boundary values to test?
self_check_questions.answer: At boundary 5000: test 4999 (BRONZE), 5000 (SILVER — careful: > not >=), 5001 (SILVER). At boundary 10000: test 9999 (SILVER), 10000 (SILVER — still <= 10000), 10001 (GOLD). Plus: 0 (minimum), 1 (just above 0), negative (should it throw?), very large value (Integer.MAX_VALUE). @CsvSource handles all 8+ cases in one test method.

self_check_questions.question: When would you use @MethodSource instead of @CsvSource?
self_check_questions.answer: @MethodSource when: (1) parameters are complex objects that can't be expressed as strings (e.g., a Product record with nested objects), (2) test data is computed programmatically (e.g., all pairs from a list), (3) the same data source is reused across test methods, (4) you need more than a few parameters and the CSV string would become unreadable. @CsvSource is simpler and preferred for basic primitive/string parameters. @MethodSource is more powerful but more verbose.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **Mocking with Mockito** — unit tests should test ONE class in isolation. But most classes depend on others (a service calls a repository, a controller calls a service). Mockito creates fake implementations of dependencies that return controlled values. `when(repo.findById('U01')).thenReturn(Optional.of(user))` — no real database needed. After Topic 12.3, you test any class in isolation regardless of its dependencies.
